critique.summary: The article introduces Manyana, a project by Bram Cohen that demonstrates a new approach to version control using CRDTs (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types). It highlights how Manyana improves conflict resolution and history management compared to traditional version control systems.
mainPoints: Manyana uses CRDTs to ensure merges never fail and always produce a consistent result regardless of merge order.
Conflict presentation in Manyana is more informative, showing what changes were made and by whom, rather than opaque conflict markers.
The system maintains a 'weave' of all lines ever added or removed, eliminating the need for common ancestor traversal in merges.
Rebasing in Manyana can preserve full history without creating fictional commit sequences, unlike traditional rebasing.
Manyana is currently a demo implemented in about 470 lines of Python, focusing on single-file operations without full version control features yet.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about CRDTs and their properties are consistent with established computer science literature on CRDTs, which guarantee eventual consistency and conflict-free merges. Bram Cohen is a credible figure in software development, known for BitTorrent, lending credibility to the technical insights. The description of Manyana as a demo project aligns with the available public information. However, the article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, limiting verification of practical usability or adoption.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential performance implications or scalability of CRDT-based version control in large projects.
There is no comparison with other emerging version control approaches or existing tools that attempt to improve merge conflicts.
User experience challenges beyond conflict presentation, such as integration with existing workflows or tooling, are not addressed.
The article does not provide independent evaluations or user feedback on Manyana's effectiveness.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the use of CRDTs in version control systems, with many commenters debating their suitability and limitations compared to traditional tools like Git. Some highlight the benefits of improved merge conflict handling and history representation, while others emphasize semantic conflicts, scalability, and practical usability challenges.

comments.author: theknarf
comments.text: You can't use CRDTs for version control, having conflicts is the whole point of version control. Sometimes two developers will make changes that fundamentally tries to change the code in two different ways, a merge conflict then leaves it up to the developer who is merging/rebasing to make a choice about the semantics of the program they want to keep. A CRDT would just produce garbage code, its fundamentally the wrong solution. If you want better developer UX for merge conflicts then there are both a bunch of tooling on top of Git, as well as other version control systems, that try to present it in a better way; but that has very little to do with the underlaying datastructure. The very fact that cherry-picking and reverting becomes difficult with this approach should show you that its the wrong approach! Those are really easy operations to do in Git.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately points out that semantic conflicts require human resolution and that CRDTs alone cannot handle all merge semantics, making it a well-reasoned critique.

comments.author: itsnexis
comments.text: The insight about rebase is underappreciated. The reason rebase is "considered harmful" in many teams isn't just that it rewrites history — it's that it makes history a lie that eventually collapses under its own weight in large teams. The CRDT weave approach is clever because it separates two things that git conflates: the logical order you want the commits to appear in (narrative history) vs. the actual causal order things happened (real history). Keeping both is strictly better. The conflict marker improvement alone would be worth the migration cost for many teams. Two opaque blobs with no attribution is genuinely one of the worst UX failures in developer tooling.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced understanding of how CRDTs can improve history representation by separating narrative and causal order, highlighting a real benefit of the approach.

comments.author: toomim
comments.text:
CRDTs actually have a long history in version control.

  - The original 1977 version control system, SCCS, was a CRDT: https://braid.org/meeting-60/sccs-is-a-time-collapse

  - It called its data structure a 'weave"

  - Brahm's old project "Codeville" used a weave for version control

  - But then git blew up in popularity.

  - The project "DARCS" tried to make a robust "theory of patches," and eventually led to the development of Pijul

  - Pijul is a VCS that is a CRDT: https://pijul.org
comments.analysis: This comment offers valuable historical context showing that CRDT concepts have been explored in version control for decades, supporting the idea that the approach is not entirely new.

comments.author: m12k
comments.text: I used to think the future of version control was semantic: E.g. I renamed a method, while someone else concurrently added another call to that (now differently named) method. Git doesn't catch this, nor would this new system. The solution seems obvious to a human: Use the new name at the new call-site too. But it requires operating at the level of the semantic meaning of a change, and not just the dumb textual changes. I used to think this would require a new version control system that encodes the semantics of the changes in the commits, in order to have them available at merge-time. But these days, it seems much more realistic to stick to git, but loop in LLMs when merging, to re-create the semantics from the textual changes.
comments.analysis: Highlights a key limitation of current VCS and suggests a promising direction involving semantic understanding and AI, which aligns with ongoing research trends.

comments.author: radarsat1
comments.text:
Is it a good thing to have merges that never fail? Often a merge failure indicates a semantic conflict, not just "two changes in the same place". You want to be aware of and forced to manually deal with such cases.

I assume the proposed system addresses it somehow but I don't see it in my quick read of this.
comments.analysis: Raises an important point that merge failures can signal meaningful semantic conflicts, cautioning against systems that mask such conflicts.

comments.author: ulrikrasmussen
comments.text: The thing about how merges are presented seems orthogonal to how to represent history. I also hate the default in git, but that is why I just use p4merge as a merge tool and get a proper 4-pane merge tool (left, right, common base, merged result) which shows everything needed to figure out why there is a conflict and how to resolve it. I don't understand why you need to switch out the VCS to fix that issue.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical solution to merge conflict visualization without switching VCS, emphasizing tooling improvements over fundamental changes.

comments.author: aggregator-ios
comments.text:
What CRDT's solve is conflicts at the system level. Not at the semantic level. 2 or more engineers setting a var to a different value cannot be handled by a CRDT.

Engineer A intended value = 1

Engineer B intended value = 2

CRDT picks 2

The outcome could be semantically wrong. It doesn't reflect the intent.

I think the primary issue with git and every other version control is the terrible names for everything. pull, push, merge, fast forward, stash, squash, rebase, theirs, ours, origin, upstream and that's just a subset. And the GUI's. They're all very confusing even to engineers who have been doing this for a decade. On top of this, conflict resolution is confusing because you don't have any prior warnings.

It would be incredibly useful if before you were about to edit a file, the version control system would warn you that someone else has made changes to it already or are actively working on it. In large teams, this sort of automation would reduce conflicts, as long as humans agree to not touch the same file. This would also reduce the amount of quality regressions that result from bad conflict resolutions.

Shameless self plug: I am trying to solve both issues with a simpler UI around git that automates some of this and it's free. https://www.satishmaha.com/BetterGit
comments.analysis: Correctly distinguishes between system-level conflict resolution and semantic intent, underscoring a key limitation of CRDTs in VCS.

comments.author: BlueHotDog2
comments.text:
This is cool and i keep thinking about CRDTs as a baseline for version control, but CRDTs has some major issues, mainly the fact that most of them are strict and "magic" in the way they actually converge(like the joke: CRDTs always converge, but to what).
i didn't read if he's using some special CRDT that might solve for that, but i think that for agentic work especially this is very interesting
comments.analysis: Acknowledges known challenges with CRDT convergence semantics, indicating the need for careful design in applying them to version control.

comments.author: ZoomZoomZoom
comments.text:
The key insight in the third sentence?

> ... CRDTs for version control, which is long overdue but hasn’t happened yet

Pijul happened and it has hundreds - perhaps thousands - of hours of real expert developer's toil put in it.

Not that Bram is not one of those, but the post reads like you all know what.
comments.analysis: Provides a corrective perspective that CRDT-based VCS like Pijul already exist, adding important context to the discussion.

comments.author: mikey-k
comments.text:
Interesting idea. While conflicts can be improved, I personally don't see it as a critical challenge with VCS.

What I do think is the critical challenge (particularly with Git) is scalability.

Size of repository & rate of change of repositories are starting to push limits of git, and I think this needs revisited across the server, client & wire protocols.

What exactly, I don't know. :). But I do know that in my current role (mid-size well-known tech company) is hitting these limits today.
comments.analysis: Critiques the fundamental suitability of CRDTs for version control, emphasizing the importance of semantic correctness and emerging AI tools.
